title: Week 1 Challenge Quiz: Think Like a Claude Power User

questions.questionText: You ask Claude: 'Help me get a job.' The response is generic. What's the FIRST thing you should improve?
questions.optionA: Switch to Opus
questions.optionB: Add more context about yourself
questions.optionC: Use Max effort
questions.optionD: Ask Claude again
questions.explanation: Better context usually improves output more than changing models.

questions.questionText: You want Claude to review your resume like a recruiter. Which technique should you use?
questions.optionA: Context Engineering
questions.optionB: Role-Based Prompting
questions.optionC: Chain of Thought
questions.optionD: All of the Above
questions.explanation: A recruiter role + resume context + reasoning gives the best result.

questions.questionText: Which user will usually get the BEST roadmap from Claude?
questions.optionA: Teach me AI
questions.optionB: Make roadmap
questions.optionC: I'm a final-year student with Python basics and 6 months to become an AI Engineer
questions.optionD: Give career advice
questions.explanation: Specific context creates personalized outputs.

questions.questionText: You're generating 50 LinkedIn post ideas. Which model should you start with?
questions.optionA: Haiku
questions.optionB: Sonnet
questions.optionC: Opus
questions.optionD: Max Effort
questions.explanation: Fast ideation tasks are perfect for Haiku.

questions.questionText: You're planning a startup, analyzing competitors, creating a GTM strategy, and estimating risks. Which model fits best?
questions.optionA: Haiku
questions.optionB: Sonnet
questions.optionC: Opus
questions.optionD: Any model
questions.explanation: Deep strategy and reasoning tasks are ideal for Opus.

questions.questionText: What's the biggest mistake beginners make with AI?
questions.optionA: Using Sonnet
questions.optionB: Writing short prompts
questions.optionC: Expecting great outputs without giving context
questions.optionD: Using Claude daily
questions.explanation: Most poor outputs come from poor context.

questions.questionText: You need a personalized 12-month AI career roadmap. Which technique is MOST important?
questions.optionA: Role Prompting
questions.optionB: Chain of Thought
questions.optionC: Context Engineering
questions.optionD: All of the Above
questions.explanation: The best outputs combine multiple prompting techniques.

questions.questionText: Which statement is closest to how AI power users think?
questions.optionA: I need better prompts.
questions.optionB: I need a better model.
questions.optionC: I need to provide better context and instructions.
questions.optionD: I need longer prompts.
questions.explanation: Context + clear instructions beat prompt length.

questions.questionText: You need a quick summary of a PDF before class starts in 2 minutes. What should you choose?
questions.optionA: Opus + Max
questions.optionB: Sonnet + High
questions.optionC: Haiku + Low
questions.optionD: Opus + High
questions.explanation: Speed matters more than deep reasoning here.

questions.questionText: After 7 days of this challenge, what's the biggest lesson?
questions.optionA: Claude is a chatbot.
questions.optionB: Prompt Engineering is enough.
questions.optionC: The right context, role, reasoning, model, and effort together create great outputs.
questions.optionD: Long prompts solve everything.
questions.explanation: Great AI results come from combining all the techniques learned so far.

title: Week 2 Challenge Quiz: Building Real-World Applications with Claude

questions.questionText: While building NutriScope on Day 9, what was the key lesson about creating large applications with Claude?
questions.optionA: Use the biggest prompt possible
questions.optionB: Generate everything in one shot
questions.optionC: Build an MVP first and enhance it iteratively
questions.optionD: Always use Opus
questions.explanation: Day 9 focused on iterative development: build first, enhance later.

questions.questionText: What was the primary purpose of Claude Artifacts introduced during the challenge?
questions.optionA: Writing longer essays
questions.optionB: Creating interactive applications and tools
questions.optionC: Training AI models
questions.optionD: Generating images only
questions.explanation: Artifacts transform Claude from a chatbot into an application builder.

questions.questionText: When generating a portfolio website on Day 10, what additional input could significantly improve personalization?
questions.optionA: Browser version
questions.optionB: Resume and profile photo
questions.optionC: Internet speed
questions.optionD: Operating system
questions.explanation: Uploading a resume and optionally a profile photo helps Claude create a more personalized portfolio.

questions.questionText: What was the most important rule while optimizing resumes on Day 11?
questions.optionA: Add more skills to improve ATS score
questions.optionB: Rewrite experience completely
questions.optionC: Never invent achievements, skills, or experience
questions.optionD: Increase resume length
questions.explanation: ATS optimization must maintain 100% factual accuracy.

questions.questionText: What was the key benefit of connecting Indeed with Claude on Day 13?
questions.optionA: Creating resumes automatically
questions.optionB: Finding and analyzing jobs based on your profile and preferences
questions.optionC: Building websites
questions.optionD: Generating certifications
questions.explanation: Job connectors allow Claude to discover and evaluate relevant opportunities.

questions.questionText: In the Job Discovery workflow, which combination produces the best job recommendations?
questions.optionA: Job title only
questions.optionB: Resume only
questions.optionC: Professional profile + job search criteria
questions.optionD: Location only
questions.explanation: Claude needs both profile information and job preferences to personalize recommendations.

questions.questionText: According to Day 14, which phrase could be a potential workplace red flag?
questions.optionA: Structured onboarding
questions.optionB: Learning opportunities
questions.optionC: Wear many hats and always available
questions.optionD: Flexible working hours
questions.explanation: Such phrases often indicate burnout risk and unrealistic expectations.

questions.questionText: Why did the Job Risk Analyzer generate interview questions?
questions.optionA: To increase ATS score
questions.optionB: To validate potential risks before accepting an offer
questions.optionC: To replace recruiters
questions.optionD: To generate cover letters
questions.explanation: The questions help candidates verify concerns discovered during analysis.

questions.questionText: Throughout Days 8–14, participants were repeatedly advised to set Claude's effort level to:
questions.optionA: Maximum
questions.optionB: High
questions.optionC: Medium
questions.optionD: Low
questions.explanation: Low effort generally works best on the free tier and reduces generation failures.

questions.questionText: What was the biggest overall lesson from Week 2?
questions.optionA: Claude is only useful for chatting
questions.optionB: AI works best without context
questions.optionC: Claude can be used as a builder, recruiter, analyst, and career assistant when given structured inputs
questions.optionD: Long prompts always guarantee success
questions.explanation: Week 2 demonstrated how Claude can create applications, optimize careers, analyze jobs, and generate professional assets through structured prompting.

title: Week 3 Challenge Quiz: Building Real Applications with Claude

questions.questionText: What was the biggest advantage of creating a Claude Skill in Day 16?
questions.optionA: Makes Claude faster
questions.optionB: Reduces internet usage
questions.optionC: Allows reusable workflows without rewriting prompts
questions.optionD: Unlocks new Claude models
questions.explanation: Skills let users save instructions and reuse powerful workflows without repeatedly writing the same prompts.

questions.questionText: In the Stock Fundamental Research Skill, which rule is MOST important?
questions.optionA: Always predict stock prices
questions.optionB: Provide buy/sell recommendations
questions.optionC: Use live data and never fabricate information
questions.optionD: Focus only on technical indicators
questions.explanation: The skill emphasizes evidence-based analysis, source verification, and avoiding fabricated data.

questions.questionText: What was the primary learning from the Vehicle Cost Dashboard project?
questions.optionA: Building mobile apps
questions.optionB: Using CSV data to generate insights and dashboards
questions.optionC: Creating social media content
questions.optionD: Training machine learning models
questions.explanation: The project focused on analyzing CSV data and generating a complete interactive dashboard.

questions.questionText: The Brain Dump Action Planner Skill is designed to transform:
questions.optionA: Images into videos
questions.optionB: Code into documentation
questions.optionC: Messy notes and transcripts into structured action plans
questions.optionD: PDFs into websites
questions.explanation: Its purpose is to organize unstructured thoughts, meetings, and notes into actionable outputs.

questions.questionText: In the Football Intelligence Hub project, Claude combined which three capabilities?
questions.optionA: Coding, debugging, and deployment
questions.optionB: Prediction, education, and personality assessment
questions.optionC: Video editing, design, and animation
questions.optionD: Database management, networking, and cloud computing
questions.explanation: The Football Intelligence Hub generated predictions, quizzes, and personality analysis in one experience.

questions.questionText: What browser API was required in the Face Puzzle Game project?
questions.optionA: Geolocation API
questions.optionB: Bluetooth API
questions.optionC: getUserMedia() Camera API
questions.optionD: Speech Recognition API
questions.explanation: The game captured the user's photo through webcam access using getUserMedia().

questions.questionText: The Digital Privacy Intelligence Dashboard emphasized which principle?
questions.optionA: Predicting exact user behavior
questions.optionB: Accessing private company databases
questions.optionC: Separating facts from estimates
questions.optionD: Tracking users in real time
questions.explanation: A key requirement was clearly distinguishing confirmed facts from inferred estimates.

questions.questionText: Across Week 3 projects, what common pattern emerged?
questions.optionA: AI was mainly used for writing essays
questions.optionB: AI was used to build complete applications and experiences
questions.optionC: AI replaced all human decision making
questions.optionD: AI was only used for image generation
questions.explanation: Week 3 focused on dashboards, games, skills, analysis systems, and interactive experiences.

questions.questionText: Why were HTML artifacts used repeatedly throughout Week 3?
questions.optionA: They require special software
questions.optionB: They allow creation of shareable, interactive applications
questions.optionC: They reduce internet speed
questions.optionD: They only work on desktop devices
questions.explanation: HTML artifacts allow users to instantly run dashboards, games, and tools in a browser.

questions.questionText: What is the biggest mindset shift from Week 3?
questions.optionA: Claude is only a chatbot
questions.optionB: Claude is mainly a search engine
questions.optionC: Claude can act as a system builder, analyst, developer, and experience creator
questions.optionD: Claude should only be used for answering questions
questions.explanation: Week 3 demonstrated that Claude can build reusable systems, applications, dashboards, and complete user experiences.

title: Week 4 Challenge Quiz: Entrepreneurship & Healthcare Simulations with Claude

questions.questionText: What was the primary objective of the Startup Validation Report created in Day 22?
questions.optionA: Design a company logo
questions.optionB: Validate a startup idea before building it
questions.optionC: Generate social media posts
questions.optionD: Create a mobile application
questions.explanation: The Startup Validation Report helps founders evaluate market demand, competition, customer problems, and business viability before investing time and money.

questions.questionText: Which framework was introduced to estimate the size of a startup's market opportunity?
questions.optionA: SWOT Analysis
questions.optionB: MoSCoW Prioritization
questions.optionC: TAM, SAM, SOM
questions.optionD: Porter's Five Forces
questions.explanation: TAM, SAM, and SOM help founders estimate the total, serviceable, and obtainable market opportunities.

questions.questionText: The Customer & MVP Blueprint primarily focuses on:
questions.optionA: Building every requested feature
questions.optionB: Understanding customers before building products
questions.optionC: Writing business contracts
questions.optionD: Creating marketing posters
questions.explanation: Successful startups first understand customer pain points, buying behavior, and priorities before developing products.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of MoSCoW Prioritization?
questions.optionA: Estimate company valuation
questions.optionB: Prioritize product features based on importance
questions.optionC: Calculate revenue projections
questions.optionD: Hire startup employees
questions.explanation: MoSCoW categorizes features into Must Have, Should Have, Could Have, and Won't Have.

questions.questionText: The Business Strategy Report generated in Day 24 emphasized which principle?
questions.optionA: Features over customers
questions.optionB: Revenue and distribution over vanity metrics
questions.optionC: Hiring before validation
questions.optionD: Scaling before product-market fit
questions.explanation: A sustainable business depends on revenue generation, customer acquisition, and effective distribution—not just adding features.

questions.questionText: Which AI judge in the Shark Tank Simulator primarily evaluates market size and scalability?
questions.optionA: Founder
questions.optionB: Customer
questions.optionC: Venture Capitalist
questions.optionD: Angel Investor
questions.explanation: The Venture Capitalist focuses on market potential, scalability, and investment opportunities.

questions.questionText: In the Prior Authorization Workflow Simulator, which three stakeholders form the core workflow?
questions.optionA: Doctor, Pharmacy, Laboratory
questions.optionB: Patient, Provider, Payer
questions.optionC: Hospital, Government, Patient
questions.optionD: Doctor, Nurse, Receptionist
questions.explanation: The simulator teaches how a Prior Authorization request moves between the Patient, Provider, and Payer.

questions.questionText: In the Prior Authorization Story Simulator, a denial means:
questions.optionA: Treatment is permanently cancelled
questions.optionB: The patient must change hospitals
questions.optionC: The provider can appeal or submit additional documentation
questions.optionD: Insurance coverage automatically expires
questions.explanation: A denial is not final. Providers can submit missing documentation or file an appeal.

questions.questionText: Which factor contributes the highest weight to the Hospital Admission Readiness Score?
questions.optionA: Bed Availability
questions.optionB: Consent
questions.optionC: Prior Authorization Status
questions.optionD: Insurance Verification
questions.explanation: Prior Authorization Status contributes 25% of the readiness score, making it the most significant factor.

questions.questionText: What is the biggest mindset shift from Week 4?
questions.optionA: Claude is mainly a chatbot for answering questions
questions.optionB: Claude can act as a startup advisor, business strategist, healthcare educator, and full-stack application builder
questions.optionC: Claude is only useful for writing code
questions.optionD: Claude replaces business decision-making entirely
questions.explanation: Week 4 demonstrated that Claude can validate startups, design business strategies, build interactive simulations, and create complete educational applications across multiple domains.
